content.text: Getting Only What Changed: Incremental Extraction & Change Data Capture

Most production data pipelines don't have the luxury of re-reading an entire database every hour. Tables can have hundreds of millions of rows; reading all of them repeatedly wastes compute, strains source systems, and makes pipelines slow and fragile. The solution is to extract only the data that has
— this is the core idea behind incremental extraction and Change Data Capture (CDC).
is the practice of querying only new or modified records since the last successful pipeline run, rather than re-fetching the entire dataset. Instead of

is a more sophisticated version of this idea. Rather than relying on columns in the data itself, CDC intercepts the database's internal change log (the transaction log or write-ahead log) to detect inserts, updates, and deletes at the infrastructure level — before any application logic runs.
These two approaches sit on a spectrum from simple to powerful:

Poll-based (timestamps/sequences) ──→ Trigger-based ──→ Log-based CDC
    Simple, broad support               Moderate           Most powerful,
    Can miss deletes                    complexity         captures deletes

A full table scan on a 500-million-row orders table might take 40 minutes and lock up I/O on the source database. Running that every 15 minutes is simply not viable. Incremental extraction reduces that to a query returning only the 3,000 rows that changed — taking seconds.
— small, frequent extractions instead of large nightly batches
— less read load on production databases
— a complete history of what changed and when
— CDC can capture hard deletes, which timestamp-based approaches miss entirely
How It Works — The Core Mechanisms
The simplest approach. It relies on a column in the source table — typically
— that records when a row was last touched.
is a stored value representing the last successfully extracted point in time. After each run, you update this watermark to
or to the maximum timestamp seen in the extracted batch.

import psycopg2
import json
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path

WATERMARK_FILE = Path("watermark.json")

def load_watermark() -> datetime:
    """Load the last extraction timestamp from persistent storage."""
    if WATERMARK_FILE.exists():
        data = json.loads(WATERMARK_FILE.read_text())
        return datetime.fromisoformat(data["last_extracted_at"])
    # First run: go back far enough to capture everything
    return datetime(2000, 1, 1, tzinfo=timezone.utc)

def save_watermark(ts: datetime) -> None:
    """Persist the new watermark after a successful extraction."""
    WATERMARK_FILE.write_text(json.dumps({"last_extracted_at": ts.isoformat()}))

def extract_incremental(conn, table: str, ts_column: str) -> list[dict]:
    watermark = load_watermark()
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)

    query = f"""
        SELECT *
        FROM {table}
        WHERE {ts_column} > %(watermark)s
          AND {ts_column} <= %(now)s
        ORDER BY {ts_column} ASC
    """

    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        cur.execute(query, {"watermark": watermark, "now": now})
        columns = [desc[0] for desc in cur.description]
        rows = [dict(zip(columns, row)) for row in cur.fetchall()]

    if rows:
        # Only advance the watermark after successful extraction
        save_watermark(now)

    return rows

# Usage
conn = psycopg2.connect("postgresql://user:pass@localhost/mydb")
records = extract_incremental(conn, "orders", "updated_at")
print(f"Extracted {len(records)} changed records")
Why bound the upper end with
If a write happens after your query starts but before it finishes, you might miss it or get it partially. Bounding by a fixed
captured at query start prevents this race condition. This technique is called a
When tables have a monotonically increasing surrogate key (e.g.,
), you can use the maximum ID seen as your watermark. This avoids timezone issues entirely.
tables (event logs, audit trails) but does not detect updates to existing rows — only new inserts.
3. Log-Based CDC — Reading the Database Transaction Log
Every major relational database maintains a
— a sequential record of every committed write. This log is the source of truth for replication. CDC taps into this log to stream changes in real time.
The three main events captured are:
— an existing row was modified (CDC gives you both before and after images)
— a row was removed (impossible to detect with timestamps alone)

Source DB                    CDC Tool               Destination
┌──────────┐   WAL/binlog   ┌──────────┐  events   ┌──────────┐
│ Postgres │ ─────────────► │ Debezium │ ─────────► │  Kafka   │
│ MySQL    │                │ (or      │            │  (or     │
│ MongoDB  │                │  custom) │            │  queue)  │
└──────────┘                └──────────┘            └──────────┘
PostgreSQL has built-in support for CDC via
. You can consume changes using the

import psycopg2
import psycopg2.extras

def stream_changes_from_postgres(
    dsn: str,
    slot_name: str = "my_pipeline_slot",
    publication: str = "my_publication"
) -> None:
    """
    Stream logical replication events from PostgreSQL.
    Requires: wal_level=logical in postgresql.conf
    """
    # Step 1: Create the replication slot and publication (one-time setup)
    # Run these in psql:
    #   CREATE PUBLICATION my_publication FOR TABLE orders, customers;
    #   SELECT pg_create_logical_replication_slot('my_pipeline_slot', 'pgoutput');

    conn = psycopg2.connect(dsn, connection_factory=psycopg2.extras.LogicalReplicationConnection)
    cur = conn.cursor()

    # Start consuming from the slot
    cur.start_replication(
        slot_name=slot_name,
        decode=True,
        options={"proto_version": "1", "publication_names": publication}
    )

    class ChangeConsumer(psycopg2.extras.ReplicationCursor):
        pass

    def process_message(msg):
        print(f"Received WAL data: {msg.payload}")
        # msg.payload contains the change in pgoutput binary format
        # In practice, use a library like 'pg-logical-replication' or Debezium
        # to decode this into structured INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE events
        msg.cursor.send_feedback(flush_lsn=msg.data_start)

    cur.consume_stream(process_message)
binary protocol is complex. The ecosystem provides better tools (covered in the Tooling section).
Before log-based CDC tools were mature, teams used database triggers to write change events to a dedicated audit/changelog table.
Your Python pipeline then reads from

def extract_from_changelog(conn) -> list[dict]:
    last_id = load_last_id()

    query = """
        SELECT id, operation, changed_at, old_data, new_data
        FROM orders_changelog
        WHERE id > %(last_id)s
        ORDER BY id ASC
        LIMIT 5000
    """

    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        cur.execute(query, {"last_id": last_id})
        columns = [desc[0] for desc in cur.description]
        rows = [dict(zip(columns, row)) for row in cur.fetchall()]

    if rows:
        save_last_id(max(r["id"] for r in rows))

    return rows
A production incremental pipeline has several distinct layers:
Applying Changes at the Destination — UPSERT Logic
Extracting changed rows is only half the problem. You also need to apply them correctly at the destination. Blindly appending rows creates duplicates; you need an

import sqlite3

def apply_upsert(conn: sqlite3.Connection, records: list[dict], table: str, pk: str) -> None:
    if not records:
        return

    columns = list(records[0].keys())
    placeholders = ", ".join(f":{col}" for col in columns)
    update_clause = ", ".join(
        f"{col} = excluded.{col}" for col in columns if col != pk
    )
    sql = f"""
        INSERT INTO {table} ({', '.join(columns)})
        VALUES ({placeholders})
        ON CONFLICT ({pk}) DO UPDATE SET {update_clause}
    """
    conn.executemany(sql, records)
    conn.commit()

from google.cloud import bigquery

def apply_merge_bigquery(client: bigquery.Client, records: list[dict],
                          dataset: str, table: str, pk: str) -> None:
    # 1. Write incoming changes to a staging table
    staging_table = f"{dataset}.{table}_staging"
    job = client.load_table_from_json(records, staging_table)
    job.result()  # Wait for load to complete

    # 2. MERGE staging into the main table
    merge_sql = f"""
        MERGE `{dataset}.{table}` T
        USING `{staging_table}` S
        ON T.{pk} = S.{pk}
        WHEN MATCHED THEN
            UPDATE SET {', '.join(f'T.{c} = S.{c}' for c in records[0] if c != pk)}
        WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN
            INSERT ({', '.join(records[0].keys())})
            VALUES ({', '.join(f'S.{c}' for c in records[0].keys())})
    """
    client.query(merge_sql).result()
    client.delete_table(staging_table, not_found_ok=True)
— when a row is deleted from the source, there is no row left to read. Three patterns handle this:
— the source application never truly deletes rows; it sets
. Your watermark query picks this up automatically.
— periodically compare a hash of the entire source table against the destination to detect missing rows. Expensive, but works as a reconciliation check.
— the only reliable way to capture hard deletes. The DELETE event is recorded in the transaction log or trigger-based changelog before the row disappears.

def apply_cdc_event(conn, event: dict) -> None:
    """
    Apply a CDC event (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) to the destination.
    event = {
        "operation": "DELETE",
        "old_data": {"id": 42, "email": "user@example.com", ...},
        "new_data": None
    }
    """
    op = event["operation"]

    if op in ("INSERT", "UPDATE"):
        data = event["new_data"]
        apply_upsert(conn, [data], table="orders", pk="id")
    elif op == "DELETE":
        row_id = event["old_data"]["id"]
        conn.execute("DELETE FROM orders WHERE id = ?", (row_id,))
        conn.commit()
State Management — Storing the Watermark Reliably
The watermark is critical. If it's lost or corrupted, the pipeline either re-processes everything (expensive) or skips data (data loss). It should be stored durably and atomically.

import psycopg2

class DBWatermarkStore:
    def __init__(self, conn):
        self.conn = conn
        self._ensure_table()

    def _ensure_table(self):
        with self.conn.cursor() as cur:
            cur.execute("""
                CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS pipeline_watermarks (
                    pipeline_name TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
                    watermark_value TEXT NOT NULL,
                    updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
                )
            """)
        self.conn.commit()

    def get(self, pipeline_name: str, default=None):
        with self.conn.cursor() as cur:
            cur.execute(
                "SELECT watermark_value FROM pipeline_watermarks WHERE pipeline_name = %s",
                (pipeline_name,)
            )
            row = cur.fetchone()
            return row[0] if row else default

    def set(self, pipeline_name: str, value: str) -> None:
        with self.conn.cursor() as cur:
            cur.execute("""
                INSERT INTO pipeline_watermarks (pipeline_name, watermark_value)
                VALUES (%s, %s)
                ON CONFLICT (pipeline_name) DO UPDATE
                SET watermark_value = EXCLUDED.watermark_value,
                    updated_at = NOW()
            """, (pipeline_name, str(value)))
        self.conn.commit()
Putting It All Together — A Full Incremental Pipeline Class

import psycopg2
import logging
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


@dataclass
class ExtractionResult:
    records: list[dict]
    watermark: str
    rows_extracted: int


class IncrementalExtractor:
    """
    A reusable, watermark-based incremental extractor.
    Supports timestamp and ID-based extraction strategies.
    """

    def __init__(self, source_conn, watermark_store, pipeline_name: str):
        self.source_conn = source_conn
        self.store = watermark_store
        self.pipeline_name = pipeline_name

    def extract_by_timestamp(
        self,
        table: str,
        ts_column: str = "updated_at",
        batch_size: int = 10_000
    ) -> ExtractionResult:
        watermark_raw = self.store.get(self.pipeline_name, "2000-01-01T00:00:00+00:00")
        watermark = datetime.fromisoformat(watermark_raw)
        now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)

        logger.info(f"Extracting {table} from {watermark} to {now}")

        query = f"""
            SELECT * FROM {table}
            WHERE {ts_column} > %(wm)s
              AND {ts_column} <= %(now)s
            ORDER BY {ts_column} ASC
            LIMIT %(limit)s
        """

        with self.source_conn.cursor() as cur:
            cur.execute(query, {"wm": watermark, "now": now, "limit": batch_size})
            columns = [d[0] for d in cur.description]
            rows = [dict(zip(columns, r)) for r in cur.fetchall()]

        logger.info(f"Extracted {len(rows)} rows from {table}")

        return ExtractionResult(
            records=rows,
            watermark=now.isoformat(),
            rows_extracted=len(rows)
        )

    def commit_watermark(self, watermark: str) -> None:
        """Call this ONLY after the destination write has succeeded."""
        self.store.set(self.pipeline_name, watermark)
        logger.info(f"Watermark committed: {watermark}")


# Example usage
def run_pipeline():
    source_conn = psycopg2.connect("postgresql://user:pass@source-db/mydb")
    meta_conn   = psycopg2.connect("postgresql://user:pass@meta-db/pipeline")

    store     = DBWatermarkStore(meta_conn)
    extractor = IncrementalExtractor(source_conn, store, "orders_pipeline")

    result = extractor.extract_by_timestamp("orders", "updated_at")

    if result.records:
        # Write to destination first
        apply_upsert(dest_conn, result.records, "orders", "id")
        # Then commit watermark — only after successful write
        extractor.commit_watermark(result.watermark)
    else:
        logger.info("No new records found. Pipeline is up to date.")
Advancing the watermark before writing to the destination.

If the destination write fails after you've updated the watermark, the pipeline will skip those records on the next run, causing silent data loss. Always write first, commit watermark second — atomically if possible.
Not bounding the extraction window on the upper end.
without an upper bound can produce inconsistent results if rows are written during the query. Always add
is captured once at the start of the run.
If client clocks are skewed, or if a batch import sets
to a past timestamp, watermark-based extraction will miss those rows. Database server timestamps (
set by the DB, not the application) are more reliable.
the watermark column to ensure pages are stable and deterministic. Without ordering, the database can return different rows on each page.
but arrive in the database due to a delayed write at
. If your watermark is already past
, you'll never extract it. Solutions include a
(re-query the last N minutes on every run) or CDC.
If the source adds a column, your
query suddenly returns different columns. Use explicit column lists, or add schema drift detection.
If your metadata store supports transactions, write the watermark in the same transaction as your destination data. This gives you exactly-once semantics.
Use a lookback buffer for safety.
Even with correct watermarking, late writes can slip through. Re-query the last 10–30 minutes on every run and rely on UPSERT at the destination to deduplicate.

from datetime import timedelta

# Instead of watermark directly, subtract a safety buffer
effective_watermark = watermark - timedelta(minutes=15)
Don't try to extract 10 million rows in a single query. Extract in batches of 10,000–100,000 rows, commit the watermark after each batch, and use pagination via the watermark column itself.
and the current watermark. If the pipeline runs but lag keeps growing, there's a throughput problem. Alert on lag, not just on errors.
Separate the extraction from the load.

Write extracted records to a staging area first (local files, S3, GCS), then load from staging to the destination. This decouples extraction failures from load failures and allows retries at each stage independently.
Postgres/MySQL/MongoDB WAL; streams to Kafka; production-grade
Code-first, watermark management built-in, minimal config
Incremental models at the SQL layer, after extraction
Pairs with Debezium; durable change event log
For transforming CDC event streams at scale
(data load tool) is a Python library that handles incremental extraction natively without boilerplate:

import dlt
from dlt.sources.sql_database import sql_database

@dlt.resource(write_disposition="merge", primary_key="id")
def orders(
    updated_at=dlt.sources.incremental("updated_at", initial_value="2020-01-01")
):
    # dlt automatically manages the watermark in its state backend
    yield from sql_database_source.orders

pipeline = dlt.pipeline("orders_pipeline", destination="bigquery")
pipeline.run(orders)
# On next run, dlt picks up from where it left off automatically
Log-based CDC requires elevated database permissions (
or specific replication grants in MySQL). Grant these to a dedicated service account with no other privileges.

Logical replication slots hold WAL segments until the consumer acknowledges them. If the CDC consumer falls behind or stops, WAL accumulates on disk and can fill the disk partition, crashing the database. Monitor slot lag with:

SELECT slot_name, pg_size_pretty(pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), confirmed_flush_lsn)) AS lag
FROM pg_replication_slots;
in Postgres 13+ to limit WAL retention.
Network failures can cause a batch to be delivered more than once. The destination write must be idempotent — UPSERT / MERGE patterns handle this naturally. Avoid plain INSERT for CDC pipelines.

When the source schema changes (column added, type changed), CDC events may fail to decode. Always schema-validate incoming events and route undecodable events to a dead-letter queue for manual inspection rather than crashing the pipeline.
Database connection strings should never be hardcoded. Use environment variables, a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault), or a
Watermark-based extraction (timestamps or IDs) is simple and sufficient for most OLTP tables but cannot detect hard deletes.
Log-based CDC is the most powerful approach — low source overhead, millisecond latency, and full delete detection — but requires infrastructure setup and elevated database permissions.
a successful destination write, never before, to prevent silent data loss.
Always bound the extraction window on both ends (lower = watermark, upper = query start time) to prevent race conditions with concurrent writes.
UPSERT / MERGE at the destination is mandatory for incremental pipelines — plain INSERT creates duplicates.
A lookback buffer (re-querying the last N minutes) is a practical safeguard against late-arriving data that slips past the watermark.
Replication slot lag in Postgres is an operational risk — monitor it actively and set retention limits to protect source database disk space.
